deck_excerpt: A Somewhat Janky Flip Effect Toolbox
deck_description: Cycle through your flip effects using the main and extra deck versions of reeshaddoll wendi, and use the WAKE CUP! cards to get them all on board! There's a very highroll world where you technically CAN establish wendikuhru+earl, at which point you have a quick effect boardwipe. This deck was built to fully exploit the Inferno Cyclone event rules while maintaining the structure of a strong NeoGoat control shell. Instead of overcommitting to FIRE gimmicks, it uses a compact FIRE engine supported by efficient staples and high-impact boss monsters.

This deck takes a completely different approach to Inferno Cyclone, focusing on control through ownership manipulation and continuous burn pressure, rather than traditional midrange play.

At its core, the strategy revolves around Lava Golem + Owner’s Seal + Mind Control / Brain Control interactions. By giving the opponent Lava Golem and then reclaiming it, the deck converts removal into tempo and pressure, while repeatedly disrupting the opponent’s field.

The Volcanic Shell + Bonfire engine once again provides consistency, enabling discard costs for Raigeki Break and maintaining card flow in longer games.

One of the most dangerous elements of the deck is its ability to create sudden win conditions through:

  • Hino-Kagu-Tsuchi (hand destruction after a single connection)
  • Megamorph, enabling explosive OTK swings after Life Point manipulation via Lava Golem
  • Tyrant Dragon, which can quickly close games once control is established

The deck also incorporates a strong stall and burn package:

  • Solar Flare Dragon creates passive damage and protection loops
  • Threatening Roar buys time to assemble combos
  • Spirit of Flames provides free bodies to maintain pressure and meet FIRE requirements

Iron Blacksmith Kotetsu adds consistency by searching key Equip Spells, mainly Megamorph, turning otherwise slow setups into immediate threats.

With Spiritualism, Giant Trunade, and multiple control spells, the deck excels at clearing the way for decisive turns, often swinging momentum in a single sequence.

While less consistent than more traditional builds, this deck thrives in a special event environment, where unfamiliar interactions and rule exploitation can overwhelm unprepared opponents.

 

Its strength lies in forcing awkward game states — and then punishing them immediately.

The core engine revolves around:

  • Green / Red / Yellow Gadget, generating constant card advantage
  • Lonefire Blossom, enabling access to Lord Poison and maintaining field presence
  • Gigantes, providing backrow pressure and explosive tempo swings

These engines feed directly into a powerful tribute lineup:

  • Thestalos the Firestorm Monarch disrupts the opponent’s hand
  • Mobius the Frost Monarch clears backrow and enables pushes
  • Jinzo shuts down trap-heavy strategies

Unlike other Inferno Cyclone builds, this deck uses FIRE monsters more as support pieces rather than its main identity, with cards like Spirit of Flames helping maintain attribute requirements without compromising consistency.

The spell lineup is focused and efficient:

  • Brain Control and Snatch Steal enable tribute plays and tempo swings
  • Smashing Ground ensures clean answers to opposing threats

Where this deck truly shines is its trap lineup, which is one of the strongest in the tournament:

  • Bottomless Trap Hole, Sakuretsu Armor, and Trap Hole provide layered removal
  • Dust Tornado offers flexible backrow control
  • Torrential Tribute and Mirror Force punish overextension

This build excels at playing a measured, reactive game, forcing opponents into inefficient trades while slowly gaining advantage through Gadgets and Monarch effects.

 

While it doesn’t abuse Inferno Cyclone mechanics as aggressively as other decks, its strength lies in consistency, solid fundamentals, and the ability to punish overextensions — making it a reliable choice in a volatile event environment.

This deck represents the WIND side of Inferno Cyclone through a fast, aggressive strategy built around Harpie synergy and continuous backrow pressure.

The core of the deck is:

  • Harpie Queen + Harpies’ Hunting Ground, enabling constant Spell/Trap removal
  • Flying Kamakiri #1, which maintains field presence by floating into key WIND monsters
  • Cyber Harpie Lady and Harpie Lady 1, providing consistent pressure and synergy

Unlike slower control builds, this deck focuses on tempo and board presence, using efficient beaters like:

  • Luster Dragon and Luster Dragon #2
  • Silpheed, which can be easily Special Summoned in this WIND-heavy build
  • Sky Scout, offering additional pressure and hand control utility

The spell lineup supports aggressive play:

  • Shrink allows favorable combat trades
  • Smashing Ground clears opposing threats
  • Snatch Steal and Premature Burial swing tempo dramatically

A key strategic choice is the use of Royal Decree, shutting down traps entirely and allowing the deck’s monsters and Hunting Ground to control the pace of the duel without interruption.

Hysteric Party provides a powerful comeback option, enabling sudden swarms and immediate pressure, especially in grind games.

This deck thrives on:

  • Constant backrow destruction
  • Fast, repeatable monster pressure
  • Maintaining tempo through floating and efficient trades

While it lacks the explosive “rule abuse” seen in many FIRE builds, it compensates with consistency, speed, and the ability to keep opponents off balance throughout the duel.

deck_excerpt: Girsu carrying 5 out of the 7 World Legacy archetypes
deck_description:
I am legitimately ashamed to say that Crusadia was the very first Deck in paper that I built where I went out and bought singles for. This Deck was not overly competent back then and it is even worse today. Crusadia is a Link-climb OTK Deck which had the majority of its archetype released in CYHO and it only needed to wait for SOFU for Crusadia Spatha and DANE for Crusadia Testament and Mekk-Knight Crusadia Avramax to round out the rest of the most essential cards for its Deck core. The Main Deck Crusadia monsters all share the same effect that allows you to Special Summon them from your hand to your Main Monster Zone a Link Monster points to in Defense Position in addition to another, different effect. The Crusadia Link Monsters have some way to gain ATK to pump themselves up, allowing you to go for an OTK, and they all also have another effect that is unique to each of them. The Crusadia Spells either help with combat or they provide much needed protection for the Deck. Shockingly, Crusadia also has 2 Trap Cards. They are meant to supplement the Deck whenever it goes first, but the Deck isn’t competent enough going first to really warrant doing so. 

Crusadia is not a difficult Deck to play, and the Crusadia “combo” where you Link-climb into an OTK is really easy to pull off. That being said, the Deck is still bad for several reasons. Probably the biggest issue with the Deck is that you have no cards in your Main Deck that actually search for a Crusadia card. The Crusadia combo relies on you drawing 1 Crusadia monster and 1 extender. That’s really easy to accomplish: You already have 5 Main Deck Crusadia names and there is a whole suite of possible extenders you could be playing. The only issue with this is that you want as many of each as possible and you also want access to Crusadia Draco and Crusadia Maximus the most. More names, extenders, and other summons allows you to play into any potential removal your opponent may have. Draco allows you to add the first Crusadia you Normal Summoned (to make Crusadia Magius) back from GY, which can add back a very important name that would still be able to Special Summon itself from hand to Linkpoint. Maximus doubles battle damage your Crusadia Link Monster inflicts when battling a monster, which becomes more and more necessary the less monsters your opponent has on board for you to attack.

This is where we get into the second issue with the Deck: it has a real issue with playing into interruption and removal. If your opponent flips a The Fallen & The Virtuous to pop your Crusadia Normal Summon, you are moving to Game 2 around 80% of the time. You really do need to establish that Normal Summon and make Magius because Magius is your only in-archetype way to search for Crusadia monsters from Deck. Magius is not the same as Magical Musketeer Max, so Link into the VRAINS! does not do anything for Crusadia. And it is even worse when you realize that your Crusadia Link Monsters don’t trigger when a monster is Normal Summoned to the zone it points to. If you do not have access to Crusadia Testament or Crusadia Power, your opponent can just remove your Link Monster or negate its effects, which forces you to have to go through the line again, and if you can’t that’s the end of your turn. 

While I have individual gripes with each of the Crusadia cards, I want to focus on one last issue the Deck has: it is the ONLY World Legacy archetype to not have a dedicated set of World Legacy Spell/Traps associated with it. World Chalice has 7 related World Legacy Spell/Traps. Mekk-Knight has 5 related World Legacy Spell/Traps. Krawler has 4 related World Legacy Spell/Traps. Knightmare is not a real Deck, but it has World Legacy’s Corruption and World Legacy’s Nightmare. Guardragon is even less of a real Deck and it has World Legacy Guardragon, which was really helpful in facilitating the archetype’s gimmick before both Pisty and Agarpain got banned. Orcust doesn’t have any named World Legacy Spell/Traps associated with it, but they just have so many in-archetype cards that interact and/or directly access World Legacy cards in general. Why is it that not only does Crusadia have no associated World Legacy Spell/Traps, but its only in-archetype ways to access ones are tied to Trap Cards in an OTK Deck? The only World Legacy Spell/Trap that Crusadia benefits from is Succession and you have to be playing Girsu, the Orcust Mekk-Knight to reliably search for it. Girsu is literally carrying 5 out of the 7 World Legacy archetypes on its back. 

Crusadia has been successful early on as a Link-climb engine in early versions of Dragon Link and Thunder Dragon, but it has never been successful at higher level events as its own strategy. Kaiju Crusadia was a version of the Deck that utilized Interrupted Kaiju Slumber as a strong going-second card to position Kaiju monsters on both player’s fields so that your Crusadia Equimax points to both, buffing its ATK to really high numbers. It could also make use of Gameciel, the Sea Turtle Kaiju and Kyoutou Waterfront to insulate against disruption going second. But, this Deck did fade away pretty quickly when going second became really difficult during the 2020 COVID-era negate-fest formats since this Deck literally cannot play Dark Ruler No More. Mekk-Knight Crusadia was another version of the Deck that made use of the Mekk-Knight monsters being able to be Summoned into your opponent’s established board and positioned so that your Crusadia Link Monsters pointed to them to also massively buff their ATK. This was worse than literal Mekk-Knight at the time, and it faded away around the same time as Mekk-Knight did. Pure Crusadia with a bunch of going second tools is probably the best way to play the Deck still. Why make the Deck more complicated than it needs to be?

This first combo will require a Crusadia name and extender where ideally one of them is either Crusadia Maximus or Crusadia Draco. Let’s say that you have Crusadia Maximus and Crusadia Arboria. Normal Maximus and use him to Link Summon Crusadia Magius. Special Summon Arboria to the zone Magius points to. Magius triggers to search for Crusadia Draco. Link Arboria and Magius into Crusadia Regulex. Special Summon Draco to the zone Leonis points to. Chain Link 1 Leonis and Chain Link 2 Draco. Draco adds Maximus from GY back to hand and Leonis adds either Crusadia Revival or Crusadia Testament from Deck to hand. If you know you can OTK your opponent this turn, grab Revival. If you can’t, grab Testament. Link Draco and Regulex into Crusadia Equimax. Special Summon Maximus to the zone Equimax points to. Activate Maximus’s effect targeting Equimax to make Equimax deal double damage when it battles an opponent’s monster this turn, but other monsters you control cannot attack. Revival gives Equimax an additional 500 ATK and can target it to allow it to attack all monsters your opponent controls once each. So, that is a 4100 ATK monster that can attack all monsters your opponent controls and deal double battle damage. If you cannot kill your opponent and searched for Testament, after damage calculation, if your Crusadia Link Monster destroys a monster by battle, you can draw cards equal to that monster’s Link Rating. You could use this in tandem with Equimax, or you can Link that Maximus into another Magius and then Link Equimax and Magius into Mekk-Knight Crusadia Avramax so that you can draw 4 off of Testament instead of 3. 

This second combo will require Girsu, the Orcust Mekk-Knight and ideally also an extender. Let’s say that the extender is Dogmatika Lawbringer. Normal Summon Girsu and activate his effect to send World Legacy - “World Crown” from Deck to GY. Activate Girsu’s other effect to Special Summon a World Legacy Token to both player’s fields. Link the Token into Galatea-I, the Orcust Automaton. Activate Galatea-I’s effect to send a card from your hand to the GY to search for another World Crown. Special Summon Lawbringer from hand because there is a monster Special Summoned from the Extra Deck on the field. Link Girsu and Lawbringer into Lib the World Key Blademaster. Activate Lib’s effect to set World Legacy Succession from Deck. Link Lib and Galatea-I into Equimax. Now, you could Succession back a Lawbringer and then Special Summon the Crown from your hand, but that only makes Equimax a 6000 ATK beater. So, Link Equimax into Crusadia Magius. Activate Galatea-I’s effect in GY, banishing it to Special Summon itself from GY to the zone Magius points to. Magius triggers to add Crusadia Maximus from Deck to hand. Link Magius and Galatea-I into Crusadia Regulex. Activate Succession to Special Summon any monster from GY to the zone it points to. Regulex triggers to search for Crusadia Revival. Link Regulex and the monster you brought back into Crusadia Equimax. Special Summon Crown and Maximus to the zones Equimax points to. Activate Revival and then activate Revival’s effect targeting Equimax. Activate Maximus’s effect targeting Equimax. You now have a 5600 ATK monster that can attack all monsters your opponent controls once each that deals double battle damage. 

For the Main Deck: 3 Mulcharmy Fuwalos and 3 Mulcharmy Purulia. I wouldn’t have even considered making this list if it weren’t for the existence of the Mulcharmies. Crusadia needs the draws to see as many cards as possible just so that it can have both its combo pieces and going-second tools. 3 Dogmatika Lawbringer. If a monster Special Summoned from the Extra Deck is on the field, you can Special Summon Dogmatika Lawbringer from your hand, and if it is Special Summoned from the hand, you can make both players send 1 monster from their Extra Deck to the GY. And if it is sent to the GY, you can target 1 Dogmatika card in your GY except for Lawbringer and add it to your hand. I consider Lawbringer to be in the same class of cards as Dinowrestler Pankratops, Kashtira Fenrir, Alpha, the Master of Beasts, and the new Revolution Cyber Dragon. They are all monsters that you can just Special Summon from your hand on your turn, they have varying ATK stats but ones that are still high enough to beat over a good amount of monsters, and they each have an effect that deals with a card your opponent controls. I really like Lawbringer for several reasons. First, it has a great summoning condition. There just needs to be a monster Special Summoned from the Extra Deck on the field for Lawbringer to be Special Summoned, and that allows it to be either used as a protective form of removal or negation (depending on what you send off of its effect) or used as an extender, which would be Summoned to a zone a Crusadia Link Monster points to either increase their ATK by 2000 or trigger their effects. That isn’t even counting the multitude of things it can do based on what monster it sends from Extra Deck to GY. Sending Elder Entity N’tss targets and pops a card on field. Mereologic Aggregator targets and negates the effects of a face-up card on the field. Garura, Wings of Resonant Life draws you a card, which is actually useful here as you want to see as many extenders as possible. Golden Cloud Beast - Malong and Tri-Brigade Arms Mouser are also valid sends, but for the sake of Extra Deck space and OTKing your opponent, you can play either Malong or N’tss and Mouser isn’t too great because you don’t inherently have piercing damage unless you target your attacker with Crusadia Leonis’s effect. 3 Ghost Mourner & Moonlit Chill. We are specifically playing Mourner because it is both disruption and it can burn your opponent. Even if this is an OTK Deck, it is not Tenpai that can guarantee an amount of damage that will kill your opponent. Crusadia relies very heavily on the monsters that your Crusadia Link Monsters point to, and it doesn’t help very much that the Crusadia Main Deck monsters don’t have great ATK stats outside of specifically Crusadia Leonis and Crusadia Maximus. Any way to get you closer to an OTK is very welcome, but this could be any of the Ghost Girl handtraps of your liking realistically. 2 World Legacy - “World Crown”. Similar to the Crusadia Main Deck monsters, you can Special Summon it from your hand to a zone a Link Monster points to in Defense. You can Quick Effect tribute it when a monster on the field that was Special Summoned from the Extra Deck activates its effect to negate the activation and destroy it. Those are pretty much the only effects of World Crown that will see use in this Deck. Its last effect is that if Crown was previously Normal Summoned/Set and Tributed, you can add 1 World Legacy Spell/Trap from your Deck to your hand. This effect will almost never be used unless you are playing a World Chalice Deck, playing Lahamu the Messenger of Sacred Scripture, or playing World Legacy - “World Key”. While Crown isn’t the worst case of a World Legacy artifact not synergizing too well with its associated archetype (I mean, it’s no World Legacy - “World Chalice” or World Legacy - “World Wand”), it is still not too great in Crusadia. The reason for that is there is no in-archetype way to access it other than your Crusadia Traps. But, for this Deck specifically, it is necessary since we are playing 3 Girsu, the Orcust Mekk-Knight. On Normal or Special Summon, you can send an Orcust or World Legacy card from Deck to GY and then if there are 2+ cards in the same column as it, you can treat it as a Tuner. Also, if you control no other monsters, you can Special Summon a World Legacy Token to both fields in Defense. Girsu is literally just carrying 5 of the 7 World Legacy archetypes on its back, and the only reason it isn’t a 7 out of 7 is because Knightmare and Guardragon are not legitimate standalone Decks. The tag team of Girsu and Galatea-I, the Orcust Automaton is definitely a force to be reckoned with, as seen by the combo I gave earlier. For the Crusadia monsters, we are playing 3 Crusadia Maximus, 3 Crusadia Draco, 3 Crusadia Leonis, and 3 Crusadia Arboria (sorry Reclusia). Each of them can be Special Summoned from your hand in Defense to a zone a Link Monster points to, which is not an activated effect. Maximus can target a Crusadia Link Monster you control to make it deal double battle damage to your opponent when battling their monster this turn, but other monsters you control cannot attack. Very good for an OTK Deck. I don’t think I could wish for something different. Draco, if Normal or Special Summoned to a zone a Link Monster points to, can target a Crusadia card in your GY other than another Draco and add it to your hand. This is an excellent effect for adding back the Crusadia name you Normal Summoned to make Crusadia Magius, which means that you can Special Summon that monster from hand to Linkpoint as it was previous Normal Summoned as opposed to Special Summoned by its own effect. Leonis can target a Crusadia Link Monster you control to give it piercing damage for the turn. This being an activated effect is kind of awkward, especially because it is on a name that normally isn’t something you NEED to search off of Magius to accomplish the rest of your Link-climb. Sometimes, the piercing really does come up. One example is when you go through the Girsu line, giving your opponent a 0 DEF Token, so piercing from Leonis and double damage from Maximus is really juicy in that scenario. Arboria can banish herself from field or GY instead of your Crusadia monsters being destroyed by battle or card effect once per turn. Sure. Doesn’t come up too often, but it is available. Notably, Arboria is also a Tuner. You cannot convince me that you are supposed to make Ib the World Chalice Justiciar with Arboria. You can do it with the World Chalice monsters because Ib is a World Chalice monster. You can do it with Girsu because Girsu is Ib’s brother. Arboria and the rest of the Crusadia monsters (excluding Avram who is Maximus and Imduk who is Draco) are the scientists who worked on creating the Mekk-Knights alongside Lee, as seen in World Legacy’s Memory. Why the hell would Arboria make Ib? The only thing she accomplished as a Tuner was turn Normal Summon Sangan into a Crystron Halqifibrax. Where’s Crusadia Reclusia? Reclusia’s unique effect is when it is Normal or Special Summoned to a zone a Link Monster points to, you can target a Crusadia card you control and a card your opponent controls to destroy them. 99% of the time, you are popping Reclusia, which actively hurts Crusadia since you literally have to hard-draw extenders and names, so trading one to pop a card isn’t exactly worth it. Also, it is a measly 400 ATK, which is barely a buff for your Crusadia Link Monsters. 

For the Spell/Traps: 1 Reinforcement of the Army to search for the Warrior Type Crusadia Arboria. 1 Triple Tactics Talent. While this could be a copy of Crusadia Power to make one of your Crusadia monsters unaffected by card effects, except its own, for the turn, I like the utility of Triple Tactics a bit more. 3 The Fallen & The Virtuous.  You can either send a monster that mentions Fallen of Albaz from your Extra Deck to the GY and target and destroy a face-up card on the field or if you have an Ecclesia monster in your field or GY, you can target a monster in either GY and Special Summon it to your field. It is really good removal, and you can add it back from GY to hand with the GY effect of Dogmatika Lawbringer. Also, you can send a copy of The Dragon that Devours the Dogma from Extra Deck to GY to search for Lawbringer in the End Phase, which may not come up often, but is still something you could do. 3 Forbidden Droplet. Being able to trade cards from your hand or field to negate the effects of face-up monsters your opponent controls and halve their ATK is really good in an OTK Deck. Originally, I was playing 3 Droplet and 3 Forbidden Crown, but I decided to Side Deck 2 Crowns instead. Keep in mind that this Deck cannot play Dark Ruler No More. DRNM stops your opponent from taking damage for the rest of the turn after resolution, which actively prevents you from OTKing your opponent, so keep that in mind. 1 Crusadia Revival, which is a Field Spell that gives your Crusadia Link Monsters 500 ATK. Also, you can target a Crusadia Link Monster you control to have it be able to attack all monsters your opponent controls once each this turn, but other monsters you control cannot attack. Great effect, but I just wish that this card also searched for a Crusadia monster on activation or something. 1 Crusadia Testament. You can choose between activating 2 different effects and you can only activate 1 Testament per turn. You can make it so that your opponent cannot activate cards or effects in response to the activation of your Crusadia monster effects this turn or you can activate it after damage calculation in which your Crusadia Link Monster destroyed your opponent’s monster by battle to draw cards equal to your Link Monster’s Link Rating. The first effect of Testament is kind of redundant considering it is much better to just play going-second tools to insulate your plays. However, the second effect is much more important. When searching off of Crusadia Regulex, you need to be able to discern if you are able to kill your opponent this turn. If you can, search for Revival. If you can’t, search for Testament. That way, you can either draw 3 cards when punching and destroying a monster with Crusadia Equimax. Or, you can Link-climb into Mekk-Knight Crusadia Avramax and draw 4 cards when it destroys a monster by battle. Long story short, this gives you the card necessary for a potential follow-up play if you cannot OTK your opponent this turn. 1 World Legacy Succession which targets a monster in your GY and Special Summons it to Linkpoint. While we can’t search this in-archetype, through the Girsu line, you can set this off of Lib the World Key Blademaster to access the entire Crusadia line off of Normal Summoning Girsu. You could play 3 copies, but it is more intuitive to just play 1 and use the other 2 slots to play going-second tools. 

Extra Deck: 1 The Dragon that Devours the Dogma which can add a Dogmatika or Tri-Brigade card from Deck to your hand during the End Phase in which it is in the GY because it was sent there this turn. Notably, we are not playing Albion, the Branded Dragon. There are 2 reasons for this. The first is that we don’t have much space in the Extra Deck. The second reason is that Dragon Devours the Dogma can search for a Dogmatika card as opposed to searching for a Branded Spell/Trap. If you are forced to go first, setting The Fallen & The Virtuous and then sending Dragon Devours the Dogma on activation allows you to search for Dogmatika Lawbringer in the End Phase for follow-up. Then, if Lawbringer is used as Link Material and sent to GY or sent to GY any other way, you can add back The Fallen & The Virtuous from GY. For the sends off of Lawbringer, we have 1 Elder Entity N’tss and 1 Mereologic Aggregator. If sent to GY, N’tss can target and destroy a card on field. If sent to GY, Aggregator can target and negate the effects of a face-up card on the field until the end of the turn. As mentioned, you could play Golden Cloud Beast - Malong instead of N’tss if you want a non-destruction form of removal. However, you could also consider cutting some other card to play a Garura, Wings of Resonant Life to get a sweet draw in a Deck that really needs all the cards it can get. 1 Mekk-Knight Crusadia Avramax. It requires 2+ monsters Special Summoned from the Extra Deck. While it is Link Summoned and on the field, your opponent cannot target it with card effects and they also cannot target other monsters for attacks. Once per battle, if Avramax is battling a Special Summoned monster, you can Quick Effect increase its ATK by the ATK of the monster it's battling during damage calculation only. Also, if it is Link Summoned and sent from field to GY by your opponent, you can shuffle a card on field into Deck. Basically, Avramax is the monster that you will go into and sit on going first if you don’t value the negate provided by Crusadia Equimax. Or when going second, Avramax is a large body with targeting protection who is especially potent when combined with Crusadia Revival and/or Crusadia Maximus and/or Crusadia Leonis. Or, if you don’t OTK your opponent, you Linkoff everything and go into it and sit on it and hope to survive. For the more standard Crusadia Link Monsters: 2 Crusadia Equimax, 1 Crusadia Regulex, 1 Crusadia Spatha, and 2 Crusadia Magius. Each gain ATK equal to the combined original ATK of all monsters they point to and monsters they point to cannot attack. Equimax is the LINK-3 and once per turn, you can Quick Effect Tribute a World Legacy or Crusadia monster it points to in order to target a face-up card on the field and negate its effect until the end of the turn. Tributing specifically a Crusadia or World Legacy monster it points to is not exactly worth it to just negate the effects of a face-up card on the field. It is okay at best considering that you are actively weakening Equimax by doing so. Regulex is a LINK-2 that searches for a Crusadia Spell/Trap from Deck if an Effect Monster(s) is Special Summoned to a zone(s) it points to except during the Damage Step. This is your only way to search for Crusadia Spell/Traps. Granted, this being the only way to do so isn’t that terrible since you only need either Revival or Testament, but it would’ve been nice if Regulex also had the option of searching for a World Legacy Spell/Trap. Spatha can target a monster in either player’s MMZ and move it to another MMZ on its controller’s field if an Effect Monster is Special Summoned to a zone it points to except during the Damage Step. Spatha’s purpose is that she picks up one of your opponent’s monsters that is in their Rightmost, Leftmost, or Center MMZ and moves it to above your EMZ so that Equimax (or Regulex) can be boosted from its ATK. It’s kind of cute in a sense. Magius adds a Crusadia monster from Deck to hand if an Effect Monster is Special Summoned to a zone it points to except during the Damage Step. It is a necessary effect rather than a great effect - is how I would word Magius. It sucks, but you gotta play it so that your Deck can actually function. 1 Haggard Lizardose which can banish a monster with 2000 or less ATK from field or GY and target 1 face-up monster on the field to make its ATK become equal to the original ATK of the monster banished for this effect. I am playing a bunch of LINK-2 monsters for the Girsu line based on what you have in your hand. Lizardose is useful in that it can banish say a Ghost Mourner & Moonlit Chill, a Mulcharmy, or a low ATK Crusadia from GY and make one of your opponent’s monsters tiny. This is great for OTKing more easily and if you use this effect on your opponent’s monster that your Crusadia Link Monster points to, your Link Monster is gaining ATK equal to that monster’s original ATK while its current ATK on field is tiny, allowing for even greater damage. 1 Lib the World Key Blademaster which can only be Link Summoned while there is a World Legacy card in your GY. During your Main Phase, she can set a World Legacy Spell/Trap from Deck, but it cannot be activated this turn if there isn’t a World Legacy monster in GY. Also, if she is Link Summoned and sent to GY as Link Material, she can shuffle a card on field into the Deck. Nearly all of the time, you are making Lib when doing the Girsu line. She sets World Legacy Succession from Deck and she is removal when she is used as Link Material. 1 S:P Little Knight. It’s a bit of a back and forth for me. S:P is better going first, but she is still technically removal that can banish a card from field OR GY when doing the Girsu line. 1 Dharc the Dark Charmer, Gloomy. In the Girsu line, you can Link Galatea-I and another monster into  Dharc and then if your opponent has a DARK in their GY, you can just take it for yourself and place it in a zone that will pump up Equimax. 1 Galatea-I, the Orcust Automaton, which can send a card from hand to GY to add a World Legacy monster or an Orcustrated Babel from Deck or GY to hand. And if she is in your GY, you can banish a different Orcust card from GY to Special Summon her from GY. She searches for World Legacy - “World Crown” and can then Special Summon herself back from GY, preferably to a zone a Crusadia Link points to so that it can trigger its effect. 

Side Deck: 3 Gameciel, the Sea Turtle Kaiju, 3 Ash Blossom & Joyous Spring, 2 Droll & Lock Bird, 1 Harpie’s Feather Duster, 2 Triple Tactics Thrust, 2 Forbidden Crown, 1 The Black Goat Laughs, and 1 Anti-Spell Fragrance. You could also consider Nibiru, the Primal Being, but be careful about the Token because its original ATK/DEF is ?/?, which is actually 0/0. So, a Nibiru Token in the zone a Crusadia Link Monster points to will not boost its ATK and you have a massive Token to deal with.

A quick draft of my first dino deck.

Does thinking 12 steps ahead sound exhausting?
Are combo lines starting to look like advanced calculus?
Did your opponent just summon half their deck and you already checked out mentally?
Did you lose AGAIN and now you’re questioning reality?
Is your brain running on 2% battery and pure spite?

 

WELL GUESS WHAT — I’VE GOT THE PERFECT LOW-IQ, HIGH-EVIL STRATEGY FOR YOU:
KASHTIRA CONTROL.
Forget “playing the game.”
Your opponent doesn’t get to play the game.
No 20-step combos. No galaxy brain sequencing. No reading essays mid-duel.
Just summon big space tyrants and start DELETING THEIR EXISTENCE.
Oh, you like your zones?
GONE.
Oh, you needed that card?
BANISHED. FACE-DOWN. FOREVER.
Oh, you wanna build a beautiful and unbreakable board for 15 minutes?
SHAME IF SOMETHING… LOCKED IT.

While your opponent is sweating through their combo lines, calculating interactions, and praying you don’t have it…
you’re just sitting there like:

“hmm yes… I won't allow it.”
sip wine.
ruin lives.
repeat.

In a meta full of combo piles, handtrap wars, and 300 IQ spreadsheets…BE the problem. Be the reason your opponent stares at 3+ locked zones while loosing LP thanks to Flare Metal Dragon and rethinks their life choices.

If these ideas and this kind of mentality appeals you... you should probably look out for help BUT there you have it: Kashtira Control (and a friend that thinks like you!) — because winning is cool…but making your opponent regret queueing up is even better!

Are you tired of getting your monsters targetted and negated by your opponent's monsters?
Are you into simplistic and efficient combos?
You don't like your opponent having fun?

Well then, this deck might be your best option! I know, I know: who plays Scareclaw in 2026?!
But hear me out for a bit; Have you ever wondered how magnificent would be to be able to just summon your link monster and floodgate the entire board without your opponent being able to respond just because you have a m*****f**** that turns all monsters in defense position and just straight up IGNORES all their effects???

It's just like "Oh, nice effect...anyway"

The combos are pretty simple use the scareclaws to summon themselves in defense position nearby another scareclaw monster and link summon into light-heart and tri-heart; meanwhile use the mitsurugi part of the deck to just do mitsurugi stuff...you know...special summon galaxy-eyes monsters? What does it even mean?

Welp, thanks to their cards Mitsus are easy level 8 spammers that will also be able to just NUKE the f****** board of your opponent; just Orochimaru from Naruto s*** I guess.

So in a world full of "Negate...Negate...Negate...Negate...Negate" be the Gigachad that doesn't give a prominent f*** and let them be your Tri-Heart's b!

Scareclaw Mitsurugi -- because sometimes the best thing is to just ignore the opponent and nuke it.

When you have Fiendsmith combo into Beatrice
deck_description:
Join the Domain Format discord server for information on rules and deckbuilding: https://discord.gg/jGkRCJkt3d

This is a deck I made thanks to my boyfriend challenging me to make Thunder Dragons work in Domain Format, since Thunder Dragon, Thunder Dragondark and Thunder Dragonmatrix can't use their effects to search other copies of themselves. My solution to this is to use the Fiendsmith engine to make Beatrice, which can send Dragondark from deck to grave, and then our deck master, Thunder Dragonduo, can banish Dragondark from the grave, which triggers it's effect to search Thunder Dragonhawk, then discard hawk to summon dark and tribute dark to summon Thunder Dragon Colossus! Dragonduo can also search any Thunder monster when it destroys a monster by battle, so it can grab one of our Blitzclique monsters, letting us trigger Thunder Dragon Titan more easily. We're playing a small Dogmatika engine to search and recycle The Fallen & The Virtuous, sending Alba-Lenatus the Abyss Dragon to the graveyard, letting us search Thunder Dragon Fusion during the end phase.
This deck is ment to quikly summon level 8 monsters so you can quickly summon your xyz monsters to win the game as fast as possable and not give your opponet a chance to set up.

deck_name: GMX x Fire King x Jurrac
deck_description:
A fun GMX variant for Genesys format. Sacrificing some Fire King engine so we can play Misc, unfortunately we have to drop Ulcanix. 

Instead we have Selande and Oviraptor for our go to normal summons, as they can both get us into our Fire King engine with Archosaur. Otherwise if we have no dino to pop we go into Megalo to set up Meteor OR Daneen to go into our GMX fusions.

The extra deck is pretty standard, Giantrainer and Ding are easy to go into as we summon a lot of lvl 8s. 

There's also a cute interaction with Horned Saurus which can place Fire King Island directly from deck which is neat. And its effect to recycle poly is quite good too.

The GMX engine also helps recycle misc from banishment which is extremely powerful.

Still some fine tuning to do but a fun starting point.

deck_excerpt: Trying to get every floodgate 100-pointed challenge
deck_description:
This deck reliably summons 3~4 rank 7s first turn, usually Chasma, Eclipse, Abyssgaios and Voloferniges

Chasma allows you to summon any floodgate monster in the game by first dumping by copying Tidal, then copying Redox during your opponent's turn to revive it. Considering that this deck's first turn is almost an autowin, this might be wholly unnecessary, but I opted to play floodgates anyway to send a message. Revive Aurora Paragon right after they used their normal summon so that it doesn't blow itself up

If you opt to not play floodgate monsters for Chasma, Copy the appropriate Dragon Ruler for extension/followup (like Tempest to search an Armed Dragon when needed), then copy blaster with Chasma during your opponent's turn for a disruption

Armed Dragons are discard fodder/extender

Disaster autowins many matchups. They will have kaijus in the side though. Little Testudo can prevent those from being summoned. Ideally you want to summon Disaster with Here There Be Dragons

Still WIP

deck_name: Seal of Orichalcos Beatdown — High Tempo Warrior Build
deck_excerpt: Seal the field. Command the warriors. End the duel.
deck_description:
Low-complexity high-tempo aggro deck.

This is a high-tempo Warrior swarm deck built around The Seal of Orichalcos, designed to overwhelm opponents through constant field presence and pressure.

Unlike typical Warrior builds, this version focuses on:
- Fast board flooding (Marauding Captain, Goblindbergh, Photon Thrasher)
- Continuous pressure rather than combo reliance
- Simple but reliable win condition

The Seal of Orichalcos turns even low-ATK monsters into real threats, allowing early-game advantage to snowball quickly.

This build prioritises consistency and tempo over complexity, making it effective and easy to pilot.

The Seal of Orichalcos is the core of the strategy, boosting all monsters and allowing the deck to convert wide boards into immediate damage pressure.

Design focus:
- High early-game board presence
- Continuous pressure
- Low brick rate

Open to suggestions on improving late-game scaling or adding stronger finishers.

This is a pure Dragonmaid deck built for casual play.

The goal is to keep the archetype as intact as possible while still maintaining consistency and smooth gameplay.

Feedback is always welcome, and I can also provide a basic guide on how to pilot the deck if needed.
A very fun deck and super competitive.

My completed Kristyasworn deck. Special Thanks to:

  • Prostorm
  • JX89
  • Cas

deck_description:
Noah Kaiba, the forgotten heir of Kaiba Corp, emerges from the depths of the Virtual World to claim what he believes is rightfully his. Unlike Seto Kaiba, who relies on raw power and dragons, Noah commands a more calculated and spiritual force. His deck blends ancient ritual power with ethereal spirit monsters, creating a strategy that shifts between control, disruption, and overwhelming presence. Every move is deliberate, as if guided by a higher intelligence beyond the physical realm.

At the center of his power stands Shinato, King of a Higher Plane — a ritual monster capable of devastating entire fields and turning the tide of any duel in an instant. Supported by divine spirits and ritual summoning magic, Noah’s strategy focuses on maintaining control while preparing a decisive strike. His monsters appear and vanish like illusions, forcing opponents into a constant state of uncertainty. Against Noah, the duel is not just a battle of strength, but a clash of intellect — where only the true heir can emerge victorious.

deck_description:
This is a high-consistency Dragon Buster build focused on reliably establishing the Buster Lock by Turn 2–3.

Key strengths:
- Multiple starter paths (Whelp, One for One, Shrine)
- Prologue engine enables instant setup
- Low brick rate vs traditional builds

This version removes slow and redundant cards to maximise reliability and control

Why this version is better:
- Reduced Buster Blader copies → fewer dead hands
- Trimmed Destruction Sword cards → less clogging
- Added Prologue engine → faster lock
- Improved spell lineup → better consistency

- High starter consistency (~80% expected)
- Fusion access by Turn 2
- Lock setup by Turn 2–3

deck_name: Dark Magician Control – Consistent Summon Engine
deck_excerpt: Turn Dark Magician into a consistent control deck.
deck_description: This deck builds Dark Magician as a consistent control strategy. This build focuses on a single goal: summoning Dark Magician early and backing it with efficient control. Cards like Skilled Dark Magician, Mystic Tomato, and Apprentice Magician improve access to your core engine, while Fiendish Chain and staple removal keep the opponent in check. The result is a smooth early game, reliable mid-game pressure, and a clear path to victory without relying on luck.

deck_name: Dark Flare Knight Engine. Consistent Fusion Control.
deck_excerpt: A consistent Dark Magician fusion engine built around Dark Flare Knight — reduced bricks, multiple fusion paths, and real turn 1 playability
deck_description:
This build works the classic Dark Magician / Dark Flare Knight strategy into a consistent, testable deck.

The focus is simple:
- Get to fusion reliably
- Always have a playable line
- Remove dead draws

Using Elemental HERO Prisma, King of the Swamp, and multiple search layers, the deck achieves ~80–90% playable opening hands and consistent access to Polymerization.

Dark Flare Knight remains the core win condition, summoning Mirage Knight for finishing pressure — this version includes redundant fusion paths and fallback plays, so you are never stuck doing nothing.

The deck also leverages Rank 4 Xyz and utility options like Relinquished Anima to maintain board presence even when fusion isn’t available.

This is not a meta deck — but it is a high-functioning non-meta system designed with engineering principles:

  • redundancy
  • reliability
  • low failure rate

If you like classic themes but hate bricking, this version is for you.

deck_name: Armed Metal Zoa – Machine Overdrive Control
deck_excerpt: Classic Machines Reforged — Explosive OTK Potential
deck_description:
This deck takes the classic Zoa / Metalzoa concept and rebuilds it with a modern, consistency-driven engine. Instead of relying on slow tribute plays and bricky hands, the deck uses Gadget and Machine synergy to maintain constant board presence while enabling powerful boss monsters like Barrel Dragon and Metalzoa.

The core idea is simple: generate advantage through repeatable Level 4 engines, control the game with efficient removal, and convert that advantage into explosive finishes using Machine-based power plays like Limiter Removal. Metalzoa remains a key win condition, but no longer a liability — it is now a reliable bonus threat rather than the only path to victory.

This deck is designed for players who enjoy classic Yu-Gi-Oh themes but want them to actually perform. It balances nostalgia with function, delivering consistent openings, strong mid-game control, and multiple win conditions.

If you’ve ever wanted to make Metalzoa viable without sacrificing playability, this is the build.

deck_name: Zera to Infinity Ritual Machine Engine
deck_excerpt: Summon Zera consistently by Turn 3 using a refined Machine engine.
deck_description:
This deck is a build of the Zera the Mant strategy.

The core idea is simple but powerful — use Machine-type Normal Monsters to fully enable Advanced Ritual Art. This deck can summon Zera consistently while still maintaining early-game presence.

This deck introduces a clean structure:

  • A focused Ritual engine with reduced dead draws
  • Machine synergy that actually serves a purpose (not filler)
  • Strong early-game monsters to avoid falling behind
  • Efficient removal instead of costly discard-based cards

The result is a deck that feels unique but functional — capable of real pressure instead of just nostalgia.

Performance targets during testing:

  • Playable opening hands: ~85–90%
  • Zera summon by Turn 3: ~60–70%
  • Low brick rate compared to traditional ritual builds

This is not a meta deck — it’s a refined concept deck that proves even older strategies can be made consistent with the right structure.

If you enjoy rebuilding forgotten archetypes into something that actually works, this is exactly that.

  • Therion "King" Regulus

  • Ryzeal Detonator with three material one of which is Eclipse Twins

At the end of your combo you can use Ryzeal Cross to target 2 "Ryzeal" cards in your GY, except "Ryzeal Cross"; place them on the bottom of the Deck in any order, then draw 1 card. Leaving you again with 5 cards in hand.

A hybrid of Yusei's standing and Turbo Duel deck using the cards he used in the third season of the anime (From the 3-on-1 duel with Rex Godwin til the reveal of Shooting Star Dragon)

Stand in card(s):

On Your Mark, Get Set, DUEL! = Speed Spell - Angel Baton

Miracle Synchro Fusion = Speed Spell - Speed Fusion

Deck is built with playability in mind, but prioritizes being source accurate, use in duels with decks of similar power level

This deck was one of the toughest to balance, as Yusei uses so many cards in this arc.

The card pool was well above 60 cards, so concessions had to be made for playability, and the side deck is used more for swapping in cards for authenticity rather than balancing. 

However, the deck has been thoroughly tested, and dead hands were rare as always, so it shouldn't need tuning as long as the M/S/T and level ratios are kept the same

A sprint-style Dark Magician deck built to end games in 2-3 turns. The core engine revolves around Dark Magical Circle and Eternal Soul working together — every time Dark Magician is summoned, Circle banishes an opponent's card, and Eternal Soul brings him back every turn to do it again. Magician's Salvation sets Eternal Soul straight from the deck, while Magician's Rod searches whatever piece you're missing. The goal is simple: lock the board turn 1 with Circle, Eternal Soul, and Dragon Knight protecting your backrow, then overwhelm the opponent with relentless DM summons and banishes before they can stabilize.

The monster lineup is aggressive. Chronicle Magician jumps onto the field for free whenever DM is summoned, boosting DM to 5000 ATK for lethal swings. Palladium Oracle Mahad summons itself the moment you draw it, and when destroyed, pulls DM out to keep the pressure going. Magician Navigation summons DM plus a Spellcaster from deck on the opponent's turn, setting up both Circle banishes and Dark Burning Magic — a Quick-Play nuke that destroys every card the opponent controls when DM and DMG are both on field.

The Link monsters serve as a flexible endgame package. I:P Masquerena enables a surprise Accesscode Talker during the opponent's Main Phase, swinging for 5300 ATK with effect immunity and unstoppable card destruction. Selene revives DM from the graveyard using spell counters, triggering Circle one more time. Linkuriboh converts spent Level 1 monsters into a repeatable defensive wall that zeros out attackers. These aren't the main strategy — the DM loop handles most games — but when you need to close out a stubborn board, the Link ladder delivers the finishing blow.

deck_name: Pendulum Scrap Engine — Consistent Synchro Loop
deck_excerpt: Turn scrap into infinite pressure — consistent Synchro loops with Pendulum recursion.
deck_description:
This deck engineers the Scrap archetype into a consistent, low-cost Synchro engine backed by a light Pendulum recursion system.

This version focuses on starter redundancy, reduced brick rates, and repeatable resource loops.

Core Strategy
The main loop revolves around:

  • Scrap Beast → Scrap Chimera → Synchro plays
  • Triggering Scrap destruction effects for value
  • Recycling monsters through graveyard and Pendulum mechanics

This creates a system where every play feeds into the next — similar to a self-sustaining engine rather than a one-shot combo deck.

Why Pendulum?
Pendulum monsters act as reusable resources, allowing you to:

  • Recover after board wipes
  • Maintain field presence
  • Extend plays without overcommitting

Key Strengths

  • High starter consistency (~80%+ access to Scrap lines)
  • Strong mid-game grind via recursion
  • Multiple Synchro paths (not reliant on one combo)
  • Budget-friendly (~$15–$30 range depending on build)

Weaknesses

  • Not meta-speed — can struggle vs heavy negate boards
  • Requires sequencing awareness to maximise Scrap triggers

Best For

  • Players who enjoy engine-based decks
  • Synchro-focused gameplay
  • Optimising systems rather than copying meta lists

deck_name: The Dark Master — Ritual Control Engine
deck_excerpt: Board-wiping Demise engine with Djinn-enhanced rituals and Zorc chaos — consistent, explosive, and engineered for control.
deck_description:
This deck engineers classic Ritual strategies into a consistent and competitive control engine centered around Demise, King of Armageddon.

The deck focuses on reliably executing Ritual Summons by Turn 2 using a streamlined search package (Manju, Senju, Preparation/Pre-Preparation of Rites), eliminating the inconsistency typically associated with Ritual-heavy builds. Demise acts as the primary win condition, clearing the board and creating immediate tempo swings that can be capitalized on for advantage or game-ending pushes.

The Djinn engine enhances Ritual Summons by granting additional effects, turning each summon into a high-impact play rather than a simple body. Graveyard setup is supported through discard outlets and targeted sends, ensuring Djinn effects are active in a majority of games.

To preserve identity and unpredictability, Dark Master - Zorc is included as a secondary win condition, introducing a high-risk, high-reward control element that can disrupt opponent boards in unconventional ways. Ruin remains as a flexible pressure option, but the deck avoids overloading on Ritual bosses to maintain consistency.

The Extra Deck is used as a toolbox, not a crutch — providing answers to difficult board states via Link and Rank 4 options without compromising the main strategy.

This deck is designed for players who enjoy:

  • Structured, consistent gameplay
  • High-impact boss monsters
  • Classic Ritual mechanics with modern optimization
  • Unique, non-meta strategies that still perform

deck_name: Ritual of light and Darkness Horus
deck_excerpt: Ritual of light and Darkness deck+horus engine
deck_description:
My take on a ritual of Light and Darkness deck. The deck throws in a horus engine to help give consitency and add some lethality to the deck's already great grind game, Plus the spellcaster ritual protects backrow, so they fit nicely together. I am new to deckbuilding so any comments are welcome!

How to play:

You want to get the trap face up using either gurifoh or the lvl 8 spellcaster. Use that to cycle for archfiend and then grab the spellcaster ritual. grab the ritual spell back from the graveyard and summon the spellcaster ritual. on the opponenet's turn use the trap to cycle for another If you open the horus cards bin horus monsters until you can find gigatrainer to refill your hand.

 

LVL 7 or higher handtraps that summon themselves can cause this deck to jumpstart on your opponents first turn, hence why fantasmy is in the side. if you get gurifoh plus fantasmy or nibiru you can start playing on your opponents first turn and reccle your handtraps!

Comments welcome, tell me what you think!

deck_excerpt: Curse of the Millennium Ring — Doom Spelled in Silence
deck_description:
Yami Bakura, the malevolent spirit bound to the Millennium Ring, duels not with brute strength, but with dark intent and cruel patience. Every move he makes is calculated to slowly tighten an inescapable curse around his opponent. Rather than overwhelming the field, Bakura drains hope turn by turn, forcing his opponent into a duel where defeat feels inevitable long before it arrives.

At the heart of his strategy lies the sinister Destiny Board, a forbidden ritual that spells out doom one letter at a time. While his fiendish monsters fill the graveyard and Dark Necrofear waits to possess the battlefield, Bakura toys with his opponent, turning their own strength against them. His duels are not battles — they are executions. By the time the final letter is revealed, the outcome has already been sealed, and the Shadow Realm awaits.

deck_excerpt: Swarm the field with 2000 ATK normals, draw relentlessly, and overwhelm opponents under the Seal of Orichalcos.
deck_description:
This deck is built around a simple but effective concept: overwhelm your opponent with high-ATK Normal Monsters backed by The Seal of Orichalcos.

Instead of relying on complex combos, this build focuses on consistency, speed, and pressure. Cards like Rescue Rabbit and Unexpected Dai allow you to quickly establish a strong board, while Heart of the Underdog provides continuous draw power due to the high density of Normal Monsters.

The Seal of Orichalcos enhances your field presence by boosting ATK and protecting your monsters, making it difficult for opponents to trade efficiently in battle. Equip spells like Mage Power further amplify your damage output, turning even basic monsters into serious threats.

The Extra Deck is streamlined for utility, giving access to Rank 4 XYZ plays and Link monsters when needed, without being required for the deck to function.

This is a clean, aggressive, and low-brick deck that performs well in casual and rogue environments — ideal for players who prefer straightforward strategies with consistent results.

Really fun deck I've been workshopping. Ecto & Spirit Monsters play really well off of each other, giving the player the option to either return the card to hand OR tribute the monster at the end of their turn, since neither effect has prio over the other in the EP. It throws people off who aren't expecting the strat. Windstorm of Etaqua is for dealing with GK Spy/other high def monsters, and if you can time it out when your opponent plays goats and you have Asura/Mataza at the ready, you'll do some massive damage.

If you face Panda/Burn/Stall decks, some of the cards (like Bottomless Trap Hole, Scapegoats, Ectoplasmer) become useless. That's when I switch in Big Bang Shot, Ojama Tokens, Spring of Rebirth, etc. for a more aggro build. BBS/Ojama/Asura can do massive damage as well.

Side note - I intentionally avoided cards like Delinquent Duo, Solemn Judgement, Trap Dustshoot, & Chaos, for no other reason aside from me finding them EXTREMELY annoying/cheesy/overplayed. There's sooooo many cards in Goat format, the fun is trying unconventional strats, even if they're not the most effective 

Have fun and please let me know if you've made any tweaks to improve the deck!

Todo is my favorite character from Jujutsu Kaisen, and this deck was made to be a perfect representation of him! The Battlin' Boxers and Gouki fit his raw melee style. Battlin' Boxer Uppercutter can search Battlin' Boxer Promoter to get 2 more boxers on field, then banish itself from grave to increase their levels and Xyz Summon Number C79: Battlin' Boxer General Kaiser to negate summons. With 2 Goukis on field, we can link them off into Gouki Sheik Ogre and summon a level 4 tuner and a level 2 non-tuner to make Gouki Dragon Ogre, search Gouki Re-Match and summon back 2 Goukis to make either Gouki The Powerload Ogre or Gouki The Master Ogre. Master is our strongest way to end the game, using it's Gouki Finishing Move, while Powerload is our unbreakable wall, with Dragon Ogre's effect making it so our opponents can only attack Powerload, forcing them to overcome it if they want to keep fighting. We're playing an Infernoble engine for a 0 card combo with Uppercutter, ending on Emperor Charles the Great, Battlin' Boxer King Dempsey with Battlin' Boxing Cross Counter set, and an S:P Little Knight. S:P's effect to banish 2 monsters temporarily represents Todo's cursed technique: the Boogie Woogie! By clapping his hands, Todo can swap the position of 2 targets by teleporting them. So by temporarily banishing monsters, that represents how the Boogie Woogie can be used to dodge attacks or retreat to safety. Other effects that represent Boogie Woogie's potential are Attack Guidance Armor and Call of the Earthbound. They switch the target of an attack, like how Boogie Woogie could trick someone into attacking their own allies! Of cource, the Black Flash has to be represented here, so we have Life Hack and Psychic Fervor to double a monster's attack power! We also have a few equip spells to represent cursed tools, with Mordschlag being a stand-in for the Playful Cloud, allowing a monster to outclass a stronger monster's power! Finally, Duel Academy is here to represent Kyoto Jujutsu High.

deck_excerpt: Playing MST in 2026? And it's actually good?
deck_description:
A year ago, if you told me that in 2026 I'd be reaching Master with Mystical Space Typhoon and Forbidden Chalice in my deck, I would not believe you. Yet, here we are!

Radiant Typhoon is the kind of deck I enjoy: interactive, not overly oppressive, decent grind game, has a variety of removal options and leaves some room for creativity and tech cards. The only negative for me is that the mirror match is painful, since your quick-plays and MSTs also trigger your opponent's effects. Still, I had a blast playing it this season!

It felt a bit weird to cut Ash Blossom & Joyous Spring at first, but with Maxx "C" at 1 the truth is, it doesn't feel that impactful at the moment. If you go 2nd with Ash as your only handtrap 80% of the times your opponent will end on the same board as if you didn't have anything (maybe even better, because you might trigger an Izuna or a Talents).

Because of that, I prioritized high impact handtraps like the Mulcharmies or Droll, as well as having plenty of decent quick-play spells. We don't have The Fallen & The Virtuous yet, so Book of Eclipse and Forbidden Chalice have to do for the time being.

In the extra deck you have some flex spots: you don't really need Downerd Magician/Divine Arsenal AA-ZEUS - Sky Thunder or Phantom Fortress Enterblathnir (I'd rather play Number 3: Cicada King or a 2nd Super Starslayer TY-PHON - Sky Crisis if I had those), so you could play something like Super Polymerization, which I did at first, but it's not that great in some common matchups like VSK9 and Dracotail, plus the discard cost is significant sometimes. You could also play an aditional Shiina, Twin Tempests of Celestial Thunder, I didn't because I didn't want to spend more UR dust (had to craft most Radiant Typhoon cards because my pulls were terrible lol).

Hope you enjoy the deck, happy dueling!

deck_description: Sun ant and Supay stuff and mixed with K9 and maybe a little dracotail for the match-up

A faithful recreation of Jack's deck from the first season of the anime (From Episode 1 up until the start of the Dark Signers arc)

Stand in cards:

Dark Hole = Speed Spell - The End Of Storm

Polymerization = Speed Spell - Speed Fusion

Archfiend's Roar = Burning Reborn

 

Deck is built with playability in mind, but priority was on being source accurate, use in duels with decks of similar power level

Some traps are situational but I kept them in because they were fun, and for the sake of accuracy all ED cards are 1-ofs

If you want to optimize the deck while maintaining deck balance, add or swap cards from the side deck, ideally keeping the same M/S/T ratios

Suggested side-outs depending on matchup are Trap Eater, Tuner Capture, and Crimson Fire.

deck_excerpt: Classic warriors, engineered for consistent pressure and controlled board dominance.
deck_description:
The deck focuses on high-efficiency 2000 ATK beaters, ensuring immediate field pressure from Turn 1. The Battleguards act as the identity core, supported by Warrior synergy, removal tools, and scalable equip spells to convert board presence into a win condition.

Consistency is reinforced through draw power and search cards, while defensive traps and board wipes provide stability against stronger boards. The result is a deck that maintains constant tempo, minimizes dead draws, and applies reliable combat pressure every turn.

Fusion is retained as a light, optional package for flexibility, but the primary strategy is clear:

Establish board → apply pressure → control trades → scale into lethal.

deck_name: Shields & Swords – DEF Conversion Control Strategy
deck_excerpt: Turn defense into offense—massive walls become unstoppable attackers with Shield & Sword.
deck_description:
nstead of relying on traditional beatdown, this strategy focuses on controlling the board early with durable DEF monsters like Labyrinth Wall, then flipping the game in a single turn with massive ATK swings.

The deck uses a defensive core to survive early pressure, supported by traps and control spells to stabilize the field. Once multiple high-DEF monsters are established, Shield & Sword enables explosive damage potential, often ending games in a single push.

A light fusion package featuring Thousand Dragon provides an alternate win condition and adds flexibility without overloading consistency. King of the Swamp ensures access to Polymerization while maintaining deck flow.

This is a non-meta, creative control deck that rewards timing, setup, and strategic play. It’s designed to feel nostalgic while still being surprisingly effective when piloted correctly

A hybrid of Jack's standing and Turbo Duel decks using the cards he used in the second season of the anime (From the end of the Fortune Cup to the 3 on 1 duel against Rex Godwin)

Stand in cards:

Dark Hole = Speed Spell - The End Of Storm

Polymerization = Speed Spell - Speed Fusion

Archfiend's Roar = Burning Reborn

Deck is built with playability in mind, but prioritizes being source accurate, use in duels with decks of similar power level

This deck proved difficult to balance due to the odd cardpool

The win rate in testing was perfectly acceptable, however key cards like Majestic Red Dragon were somewhat tough to summon despite Red Dragon Archfiend being much easier to bring to the field

If you want to optimize the deck while maintaining deck balance, add or swap cards from the side deck, ideally keeping the same M/S/T ratios

Unlike Yusei's deck, Jack has a much higher number of situational traps, so many are kept in the side deck for convenience

Cards that should be sided in/out depending on matchup: Trap Eater, Tuner Capture, and Crimson Fire

deck_excerpt: A good introduction to modern YGO
deck_description:
Mitsurugi is a Ritual Deck, the core gameplan involves finding the combination of a Ritual Monster and a Ritual Spell and then Tributing monsters to summon a powerful boss, in this case Mitsurugi Murakumo. 

Mitsurugi illustrates a lot of what modern YGO looks like. Once the main gameplan is enacted Mitsu gains value from adding several cards from deck to hand and continually bringing back its Ritual bosses from the GY,
It's just you fusion summoning as often as possible. That's all

deck_excerpt: Taking the best of Mitsu and Striker and mashing them together.
deck_description:
As a deck, Mitsurugi is very well positioned to be paired with a variety of other strategies. Throughout the deck's existence in the metagame it sometimes succeeds alone but often thrives when another engine is supplimenting the deck. In this case, we've gone with Sky Striker as the pairing because the cost-to-power ratio is crazy. Some engines are very expensive that go alongisde Mitsurugi and that's fine, but Striker managaes to be both affordable and powerful which is a great combo. Don't think Striker is the "weaker budget version" this deck has real results behind it. 

Sky Striker as a deck is a control & board breaking gameplan, the Sky Striker cards excel at getting value and cracking through the opponent's field. Mitsurugi is already good at board breaking, so this deck ends up being even better at it. However, the Sky Striker cards contribute much needed draw power and layering that Mitsurugi appreciates.

A faithful recreation of Crow's standing and Turbo duel decks from the second season of 5Ds (from his first appearance after the Fortune Cup until the 3-on-1 duel with Rex Godwin)

Stand in cards:

Dark Hole = Speed Spell - The End Of Storm

Star Changer = Speed Spell - Tune Up 123

On Your Mark, Get Set, DUEL! = Speed Spell - Angel Baton

This series of decks is usually dedicated to balancing the sheer number of cards in characters' decks so that character duels are more playable while still being accurate, but Crow's deck from this period presented a unique issue, as he only barely has enough cards for a full deck with decent ratios before Godwin's defeat, so every monster needed to be ran at 3-of

The deck is still very playable despite this issue, almost being too strong for its period, while also having slight consistency issues when it comes to Synchro summons

To maintain balance with other decks of this period while still remaining playable, removal cards were kept at a minimum, and the side deck is limited to only cards that would be added in place of monsters to reduce unplayable hands (as few as they are) without increasing the power of the deck itself

Suggested side-out options: Fane The Steel Chain, Elphin The Raven, and Scirocco The Dawn

Deck idea for a possible future Duel Links character Damon Lopez (ARC-V). I hope the KONAMI think kinda similar way as me :)

He only used the "Alien Shocktrooper" monster. So that inspired me to add him the "Alien" archetype with other UFO like cards.

(Ace animated monster: none)

This is a work in progress deck as it still has some bottle necks along with some kinks to be worked out.

What I know:
Aside from the standard new phantom knights line, you need to run triple of the new boots phantom knight as he gets special summoned no matter what one way or another.
You need to run The Fallen & The Virtuous as it can pop your own phantom knight xyz monsters, to float/extend into other things.
Thus me running the radiant typhoon package.
When going burning abyss route, try to use them as xyz marterial for the leviair's as when you double xyz with the two extender leviairs for the annoying F0, it counts as sending Cir, Malebranche of the Burning Abyss to the graveyard getting yet another extension.
Bamboo can fuel F0 after being an omni draw.
You should really only run one of each of the extenders, including the new trap. Outside of being awesome, it doesn't really come into play that much. You can run two but.... I would not because I think radiant has better recursion.
Main reason I run F0 is because this card is so annoying to get rid of, and it cannot be destroyed by card effects, meaning you can heavenly kings dragon, target your own guy to get a free pop. 

Things I am still working out:
The radiant typhoon link blocks up the link spot and makes it hard to continue combo-ing. You can place it and another of your monsters in the S/T zone to free up space for burning abyss extenders, then you can. You actually have enough gas to do so. It just feels like a waste... but you do get that nice trap out... I dunno
Maybe running two of the new xyz phantom knights scythe card is good? You do rely on it a lot to either get torn scales or the new boots...
Maybe the xyz dragon cards are not good enough? you do have to use up a spot in your deck for the old rank up to set on your op's turn after going into Dark Rebellion Xyz Dragon of the Four Heavenly Dragons, then going into requiem, but I dunno if it is worth it... maybe there is something better.
Maybe don't run wings... but maybe there is some way to run I:P or something else to make use of these extenders on your op's turn? I usually use the trap guy on my turn... Honestly maybe cut the wing and run another trap guy: The Phantom Knights of Umbrage Veil. That way you can do more with your things on your opp's turn.
Maybe when you do that you run more dark xyz monsters, but I don't know if there are many great options.

Anyway, just some shower thoughts. Please comment to help the deck! I need more ideas and more testers!

deck_excerpt: Remember in the anime that Magnet Warriors defeated all three Egyptian Gods? You can now do similar feats in Masterduel!
deck_description:
Remember in the anime that Magnet Warriors defeated all three Egyptian Gods? You can now do similar feats in Masterduel! Now you can harness the power for yourself now that the new Magnet support that has been released in Masterduel!

Magnet Warriors feature many in-archetype interruptions such as Tellusion's targeted destruction and Flux Oschenfeld's targeted bounce. can also field the dreaded Naturia Beast, granting the strategy unlimited spell negates! The deck also has a ton of 1-card and 2-card starters (about 17) so it has a lot of room for staples and consistency boosters! Even if you draw multiple starters, they also act as extenders to your combo, giving Magnet Warriors a lot of pushes to plow through your opponent's interruptions.

The new cards the deck obtained are some of the most pushed cards ever printed, such as Conduction Warrior Linear Magnum Plus Minus, which is a double foolish burial for cost! And it's effect to summon itself isn't even acitvated, so it's extremely hard to negate! It also sports a 2700ATK body which can further boost itself, allowing the card to blast through your opponent's monsters to break through their board!

Magnet's premier quick-play spell Is also extremely good, allowing for searching & quick fusion even during your opponent's turn! It also has "bonding" in it's name, which has coincidental synergy with Duoterion since it can search it!


Here is the 1-card combo with Conduction Warrior Linear Magnum Plus Minus:

1. Summon Conduction Warrior Linear Magnum Plus Minus sending from deck to GY Magnet Warrior Sigma Plus and Beta the Electromagnet Warrior. 

2. Activate Sigma Plus' GY effect to summon Beta from GY. 

3. Beta's on-summon effect searches for Sigma Minus. 

4. Activate Sigma Minus' effect to fusion summon using itself + Conduction Warrior Linear Magnum Plus Minus to fusion summon Conduction Warrior Plasma Magnum. 

5. Activate Plasma Magnum's on-summon effect to search for Magnet Bonding from deck. 

6. Activate Magnet Bonding to search for Epsilon the Magnet Warrior. 

7. Activate Plasma Magnums effect sending from deck to GY Tellusion the Magna Warrior for an extra normal summon to summon Epsilon. 

8. Activate Epsilon's effect to send from deck to GY Magnet Warrior Omega Plus to summon from GY Sigma Minus. 

9. Sigma Minus' on-summon effect sends from deck to GY any level 8 Magnet Warrior (either Alpha or Berserkion. Potenial fusion material if you have another Magnet Bonding). 

10. XYZ summon Epsilon + Sigma Minus for Gallant Granite. 

11. Detach an Sigma Minus from Gallant Granite to search for Adamancipator Researcher from deck (if you already have her search Nibiru or follow-up). 

12. Special summon Researcher from hand. 

13. Synchro summon Naturia Beast using Researcher + Beta. 

14. Summon from GY Tellusion the Magna Warrior banishing Sigma Minus and Sigma Plus. 

Channel Yugi and Bastion's power of magnetism with these magnet mechas and absolutely dominate Masterduel with the powerful & consistent Magnet Warriors! 

Want to see the deck in action? Scroll to the very bottom for the Youtube video!

GLHF DUELISTS!

deck_excerpt: Ritual. Pendulum. Archfiends. 3 wonderful parts of Yugioh all rolled into 1. Wait... Highness is a LIGHT Fiend?.. a Level 3 LIGHT Fiend?!!
deck_description:
This is based on my new latest pet deck for locals, Ritual Pendulum Archfiends! 

The new Archfiend support really ties everything together, but Fiendsmith the main consistency and grind engine of the deck. Tour Guide is the strongest play in the deck, and gets the entire deck rolling if uninterrupted.

For example: [normal summon Tour Guide > summon Buio > Xyz into Melomelody > Link into Requiem > add Mutiny in the Sky] is a 1-card combo that can turn into Fiendsmith Sequence + Necroquip + Aerial Eater + Archfiend Strategy + both new Ritual Pendulum Archfiends in hand ready for a pendulum summon with scales 0 and 9.

Regenesis is here because although most any Archfiend offers great domains for the deck, I needed the extra domain to play Sage for the negate. Also, Regenesis Archfiend's summoning condition plays well with all the 2500 ATK monsters, so I figured that adding more of the archetype would give a better chance of opening with the required monster. On top of that, Regenesis also has a very good grind game with powerful disruptions and beatdown.

Unchained here is the key to a few strategies. For generic GY recursion, Yama revives almost anything, and Rage/Anguish add back any Fiend that has found its way to the GY. The other reason is to trigger Pandemonium as many times as physically possible. The main deck monsters each have some way to destroy an Archfiend, and Unchained Abomination + Archfiend Playtime with an face-up Ritual Pendulum Archfiend in the Extra Deck is a roadmap to advantage city.

The Ritual of Light and Darkness... I love this Archetype. Despite BLS not being DARK. Fortunately, that works in our favor here since Regenesis Archfiend is also EARTH. In non-singleton versions of this deck, I prefer to just run 2 Skull Archfiend of Chaos for the unmatched utility of recycling ANY cards from GY/banishment and the perfect compliment to Preparation of Rites. Even if Skull Archfiend gets banished, Archfiend Emperor can bring it back once he is in the EMZ. However. I needed another on-theme card to recycle for Skull Archfiend, the Spell/Traps are actually quite good interaction, Gurifoh is a Fiend, and BLS is EARTH. So here we are. 

You might be wondering why I chose to include Shipping Archfiend. Besides the fact that I find the card itself to be hilarious, it is also the easiest way I can think of to retrieve the Pendulum Rituals to the hand once they hit the Extra Deck. The recycling effect of Doom Regina is extremely useful, and Archfiend Usurpation is a fantastic toolbox card. Another way is to use Mind Shuffle, but that requires you to have 1 of 3 possible monsters in hand in a singleton deck (albeit 1 is highly searchable due to including "Archfiend" in the name).

That's the most of it. There are definitely a few sub-optimal picks, and I would wholeheartedly love to include Resonators and RDA in the deck, but the Extra Deck is simply too tight to fit enough pay-offs for locking into Synchros for the rest of the turn in this version. Luce, Caesar, and A Bao might seem like easy flex spots but they are all strong interuptions, are important for grind game, and make great boss monsters, especially under Archfiend Strategy or Palabyrinth. The eventual goal after doing some testing is to include a Synchro or 2 (probably either Chaos Angel or Fabled Levilazebul), and add a couple more Fiend pendulums like Hallohallo and Gallugallud the Endless Enigma. Gallugallud is a decent Advanced Ritual Art target for Matador and has an excellent scale.

Archfiends are incredibly flexible if you can manage to avoid the locks on Royal and Highness, so try swapping some stuff out until it suits your tast. Enjoy!
